title: Spotting Money Scams
summary: First-time earners are prime targets for scams: fake 'guaranteed return' schemes, loan-app traps, and pushy tips online. A few simple rules protect you from most of them.

sections.heading: If it's guaranteed and high, be suspicious
sections.body: Real investments don't promise high guaranteed returns. 'Double your money', 'assured 5% a month', or pressure to 'invest now before it's gone' are classic red flags of a scam or Ponzi scheme.

sections.heading: Tips are not advice
sections.body: Social-media 'finfluencers' may be paid to promote products, and most are not registered advisers. Be especially wary of anyone selling a specific stock, fund, or course as a sure thing.

sections.heading: Protect your details
sections.body: Never share OTPs, passwords, or card details. Be careful with unknown loan apps that demand huge permissions. When unsure, slow down — scammers rely on urgency and pressure.

quiz.prompt: Which is the biggest red flag of a scam?
options: A 'guaranteed' high return with urgency to act now
An adviser who explains the risks

quiz.prompt: Someone calls asking for the OTP sent to your phone. You should:
options: Never share it — no genuine service needs your OTP

quiz.prompt: A social-media 'finfluencer' pushing a specific stock as a sure thing should be treated as:
options: A registered adviser you can fully trust
Someone who may be paid to promote it and is often not a registered adviser
Always correct because they have many followers

quiz.prompt: Scammers most often rely on which tactic to push you?
options: Urgency and pressure to act now
Giving you weeks to decide calmly

quiz.prompt: An unknown loan app demands huge permissions on your phone. The safe response is to:
options: Grant everything so the loan is approved faster
Be wary and avoid it — such demands are a red flag
Share your card details to speed it up

disclaimer: Educational content only. This is a simulation, not financial advice.
